items.q: What is reduction in terms of electrons?
items.correct: Gain of electrons (the oxidation number is reduced).
items.misconception: Reduction means losing oxygen and oxidation means gaining oxygen — full stop.
items.random_wrong: Reduction means a substance becomes physically smaller in size.
items.why: Students learn the oxygen definition first (from rusting/combustion) and never update to the more general electron-transfer definition.

items.q: What does a mole of a substance represent?
items.correct: Avogadro's number (6.02 x 10^23) of particles of that substance.
items.misconception: A mole is a unit of mass equal to the relative molecular mass in grams of any substance.
items.random_wrong: A mole is a unit of volume equal to 24 dm^3 at any temperature.
items.why: Students conflate the mole (a count) with molar mass (a mass-per-mole), because in practice they use moles to convert to grams.

items.q: At room temperature and pressure, what volume does 1 mole of any gas occupy?
items.correct: Approximately 24 dm^3 (24,000 cm^3), regardless of the identity of the gas.
items.misconception: Heavier gases occupy a smaller volume per mole because their particles are denser.
items.random_wrong: Different gases occupy completely different volumes proportional to their molar mass.
items.why: Students assume heavier particles must be more compact, missing that gas volume depends only on number of particles, T and P (Avogadro's law).

items.q: The oxidation state of Mn in MnO₄⁻ is:
items.why: It's +7. “+4” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: A cell has E°(cathode) = +0.80 V and E°(anode) = −0.40 V. The EMF is:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: +1.20 V. “+0.40 V” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
